Name: RateDistortionOptimalCompression
Title: Rate-Distortion Optimal Compression for Deep Neural Networks
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the optimal compression of deep neural networks can be achieved by formulating the problem as a rate-distortion optimization, where the goal is to minimize the distortion in model performance while reducing the bit-rate of the model. This approach will leverage information-theoretic principles to derive achievable and converse bounds, and will provide a principled way to allocate bits to different layers and weights based on their task-relevant information content.
Related Work: Recent works such as 'Spectral Quantization Routing' [1] and 'Training-Free Geometric Interference Cancellation' [2] have explored dynamic precision allocation and interference cancellation using geometric diagnostics. However, these methods do not focus on the fundamental limits of compression and do not provide a unified rate-distortion framework. Our work aims to bridge this gap by deriving theoretical bounds and practical algorithms for optimal compression.
Abstract: We propose a novel approach to model compression that formulates the problem as a rate-distortion optimization. By leveraging information-theoretic principles, we aim to minimize the distortion in model performance while reducing the bit-rate of the model. Our method involves deriving achievable and converse bounds for the compression of deep neural networks and providing a principled way to allocate bits to different layers and weights based on their task-relevant information content. We validate our approach through experiments on standard benchmarks, demonstrating superior compression efficiency and minimal performance degradation compared to existing methods.
Experiments: Derive the rate-distortion function for a given deep neural network model, using mutual information as the distortion measure.
Implement a quantization algorithm that allocates bits to different layers and weights based on the derived rate-distortion function.
Compare the performance of the proposed method with existing compression techniques (e.g., uniform quantization, pruning, and mixed-precision) on standard benchmarks such as CIFAR-10 and ImageNet.
Evaluate the robustness of the compressed models under distributional shifts and noisy environments.
Conduct a theoretical analysis to establish the convergence and stability of the proposed compression algorithm.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The derivation of the rate-distortion function may require strong assumptions about the distribution of the model weights and activations, which may not always hold in practice.
The computational complexity of the proposed method may be higher compared to simpler heuristics, especially for large-scale models.
The performance of the compressed models may degrade under extreme distributional shifts or noisy conditions, which may limit the applicability in certain real-world scenarios.
